mainEntity.name: Can I get workers comp for carpal tunnel even if I didn't have a specific 'accident'?

acceptedAnswer.text: Yes, carpal tunnel is a recognized occupational disease if it results from repetitive work duties. You do not need a single accident event, but you do need a medical diagnosis that links the condition specifically to your repetitive tasks at work.
acceptedAnswer.description: Repetitive motion injuries like carpal tunnel or tendonitis are classic occupational diseases. However, insurers hate these claims because they are "subjective." To win, you need to show your job required specific, high-frequency movements (e.g., 8 hours of typing or assembly line work). If you didn't report it immediately, the insurer will claim it happened during your time off. In Boise and Salt Lake, we recommend reporting the pain the moment it begins to interfere with your work. Even if you "pushed through" for a year, you can still file, but we will have to explain the delay as "gradual onset." Early intervention with a lawyer helps frame the report correctly from day one.
